text:
Your working directory is a checkout of the real react-datepicker repository (a React date-picker component library) at the commit where the bug below reproduces. Find and fix the bug in the library source.

Bug: `DatePicker` accepts an explicit `timeZone` prop (an IANA zone name such as "Pacific/Kiritimati") meant to pin the picker to a timezone different from the browser's local timezone. When that `timeZone` differs significantly from the local/browser timezone, the picker gets the calendar day wrong in three places: (1) clicking a day in the calendar grid produces an `onChange` date, and a visually 'selected' day, that are off by one day (in the configured timeZone) from the day the user actually clicked; (2) the formatted value shown in the text input is off by one day because it reflects UTC/browser-local time instead of the configured timeZone; (3) with `selectsMultiple`, the calendar does not consistently highlight the correct set of days as selected in the target timezone. In short, calendar day-highlighting, the input's formatted display value, and multi-date-selection highlighting must all consistently interpret `selected` / `startDate` / `endDate` / `selectedDates` (and the values handed to `onChange`) in terms of the configured `timeZone`, not the browser's local timezone. Fix the library source (under `src/`); do not just add workarounds in test files.

Environment notes: this is a Node/TypeScript project managed with Yarn (Berry) via Corepack; dependencies are already installed (re-run `corepack enable && yarn install --immutable` if you ever need to). Tests use Jest — run a single file with `node_modules/.bin/jest <path>`.

`--dangerously-bypass-hook-trust` is enabled. Enabled hooks may run without review for this invocation.

text: I’ll trace how `timeZone` flows through `DatePicker`, calendar selection, input formatting, and multi-date highlighting, then patch the shared date normalization points and run focused Jest coverage plus type/build checks where available.

text: The repository already has partial timezone support in `src/index.tsx`, including conversion on click and zoned preselection, so the likely defect is inconsistent normalization: some child components still compare raw `Date` objects in browser-local calendar terms. I’m reading those paths and the existing timezone tests before editing.

input.command: /bin/bash -lc "sed -n '1,220p' src/date_utils.ts && rg -n \"toZonedTime|fromZonedTime|formatMultipleDates|safeDateFormat|isSameDay|isDate\" src/index.tsx src/month.tsx src/day.tsx src/date_utils.ts"
/bin/bash -lc "sed -n '420,525p' src/date_utils.ts; sed -n '730,765p' src/date_utils.ts; sed -n '210,245p' src/date_utils.ts; sed -n '1,80p' src/index.tsx"
/bin/bash -lc "rg -n \"formatDate\\(|formatInTimeZone|dateFormat\" src/date_utils.ts | head -30; sed -n '390,450p' src/date_utils.ts; sed -n '1090,1235p' src/index.tsx"

text: The fix needs two coordinated layers. Calendar-facing values should be converted to “wall-clock” dates in the configured zone before `Month`/`Day` perform calendar comparisons, while `DatePicker`’s callbacks and input formatting must continue converting selected wall-clock dates back to real instants and formatting instants in the target zone. I’m applying that at the existing utility and `renderCalendar` boundaries so range and multiple modes share the same behavior.

I’m editing `date_utils.ts` to make the existing safe formatters timezone-aware, and `index.tsx` to normalize calendar props and internal comparisons. The important ordering change is that a clicked date remains a target-zone wall-clock date throughout picker state and comparisons; conversion back to an instant happens only at the `onChange` boundary.

The core paths are now patched, and focused tests are running. I found two cleanup details during review: range input formatting must receive the timezone too, and `onSelect` should preserve the prior contract of receiving the converted instant rather than the internal wall-clock date. I’m correcting those before type-checking.

text: The first verification pass caught only compile-level issues from the refactor: one callback reference escaped its local scope, and the formatter’s locale type is intentionally broader than `date-fns-tz`’s locale type. I’m fixing those directly, then I’ll add a focused regression test for the extreme timezone and run the affected suite again.
